answer:
In Allama Iqbal's *Bang-e-Dara* (The Call of the Marching Bell), divided across three parts in this corpus, *hayat* (translated as "life") transcends mere biological existence. It signifies a dynamic, divine-infused spiritual vitality—a fiery, harmonious force derived from God (often symbolized as the Sun)—that animates creation, empowers the self (*khudi*), and defies death and despair. This contrasts transient mortal "life," subject to decay, with "true life" as eternal, purposeful striving aligned with Islamic faith and cosmic order.[^1][^2][^3][^4]

### Vital Force from Divine Light
In "Aaftab" (The Sun), the Sun embodies *hayat* as the originating "fire and harmony" stabilizing all existence and granting breath to living things, portraying it as incarnate divine consciousness rather than passive survival.[^5]

The bud in "Kali" (Part 2) craves this solar *hayat* as an inner "fire of life" igniting every atom of the soul, mirroring the seeker's yearning for transcendent vitality.[^6]

### True vs. Mortal Hayat
Part 1's "Subah Ka Sitara" (Morning Star) explicitly distinguishes "*true life*"—immune to "Death's decree"—from illusory mortal life that "demands its fee," elevating *hayat* to an undying state of higher purpose.[^7]

### Resilience in Struggle
In Part 3's "Har nafas Iqbal tera aah mein mastoor hai" (The Muslim), *hayat*'s "mysteries" reveal themselves to the faithful, who reject despair amid "*life's great struggle*" (*paikaar-e-hayat*), trusting divine destiny.[^8]

This motif recurs thematically across the cycle, linking *hayat* to self-realization, communal revival, and eternal renewal, though not exhaustively in every poem.[^9]

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch.42 ("Subah Ka Sitara"), [translation/](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/42-subah-ka-sitara/translation/).

[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch.16 ("Kali"), [translation/](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/16-kali/translation/).

[^3]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch.10 ("Har nafas Iqbal tera aah mein mastoor hai"), [translation/](works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/10-har-nafas-iqbal-tera-aah-mein-mastoor-hai/translation/).

[^4]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, wiki card, work-level chapter map.

[^5]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch.14 ("Aaftab"), [p-2f123e](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/14-aaftab/translation/#p-2f123e) and [p-abf68a](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/14-aaftab/translation/#p-abf68a).

[^6]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch.16 ("Kali"), [p-076506](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/16-kali/translation/#p-076506).

[^7]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch.42 ("Subah Ka Sitara"), [p-e76893](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/42-subah-ka-sitara/translation/#p-e76893).

[^8]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 3*, ch.10 ("Har nafas Iqbal tera aah mein mastoor hai"), [p-7ba1e4](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/10-har-nafas-iqbal-tera-aah-mein-mastoor-hai/translation/#p-7ba1e4).

[^9]: Corpus search for "hayat" (transliteration) and wiki cards confirm these as primary loci; no broader statistical refrain emerges across all 100+ poems.
